abstract: Measuring the degree of similarity between textual units is a fundamental component of various Natural Language Processing tasks, ranging from information retrieval and document clustering to machine translation and automatic essay grading. This paper provides a comprehensive survey of text similarity approaches, categorizing them into three primary frameworks: string-based, corpus-based, and knowledge-based measures. String-based metrics evaluate lexical similarity through character-level and term-level comparisons. Corpus-based approaches utilize statistical information derived from large-scale text collections to capture semantic resonance, while knowledge-based methods leverage organized semantic networks, such as WordNet, to quantify relationships between concepts. In addition to individual methodologies, the survey examines hybrid models that integrate multiple techniques to achieve higher accuracy. By consolidating the most prominent algorithms and existing software packages, this survey serves as a technical roadmap for researchers and practitioners working on semantic and lexical text analysis.
